risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Block randomisation will be undertaken using opaque envelopes, so that blocks of six to seven participants (two to three for each intervention group and two to three for control group) will be randomized at a time." Comment: The study explicitly states block randomisation with opaque envelopes, which indicates a systematic random allocation process rather than a predictable non-random rule. Although the exact random number generation method is not described, the reported procedure is sufficient to suggest proper random sequence generation.

Quote: "Block randomisation will be undertaken using opaque envelopes, so that blocks of six to seven participants ... will be randomized at a time." Comment: The paper mentions "opaque envelopes" but does not state that they were sequentially numbered and sealed, nor that allocation was centrally controlled or otherwise concealed from the recruiter. Because the concealment mechanism is not described with enough detail to ensure that foreknowledge of the next assignment was prevented, allocation concealment remains unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "As participants will either be doing one of two different exercise programs or not doing an exercise program, assessors and participants will not be blinded to their respective group allocation." Comment: Participants and personnel delivering the intervention were not blinded, and this could plausibly affect subjective and self-reported outcomes here (e.g., pain, function, quality of life, perceived improvement) as well as adherence and reporting of exercise-related effects, so the dominant risk for performance bias is high.

Quote: "As participants will either be doing one of two different exercise programs or not doing an exercise program, assessors and participants will not be blinded to their respective group allocation." Comment: The study explicitly states that assessors were not blinded. The key outcomes for this review include WOMAC pain, WOMAC function, and AQoL, which are patient-reported and therefore susceptible to detection bias when participants/assessors know allocation. Although some performance tests are more objective, the presence of unblinded assessors and subjective self-reported outcomes means outcome measurement could be influenced by knowledge of group assignment.

Summary: This is a protocol paper describing a planned pilot RCT, not a completed results paper. It states that feasibility outcomes will include "percentage of participants who complete the intervention" and "number of participants who dropout," but it does not report any actual attrition, withdrawals, exclusions after randomisation, or handling of missing outcome data. Comment: Because no post-randomisation attrition data or missing-data method is reported, the risk of bias from incomplete outcome data cannot be judged from the available text.
